title: Queen of Puddings
subtitle: Baked breadcrumb custard spread with jam and finished with a golden meringue
excerpt: Three layers built in sequence: a baked custard made with breadcrumbs, warm raspberry jam spread on top, then a meringue piled high and returned to the oven until golden. A Victorian showpiece that is easier to make than it looks.
sourceNotes: Queen of Puddings appears in Victorian cookery writing including Mrs Beeton's household management guides. It was designed as an economy pudding that used stale bread and a small amount of jam to produce something that looked considerably more impressive than its ingredients suggested.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The custard base can be baked a day ahead and kept in the fridge. Add the jam and meringue on the day, then bake the meringue fresh before serving.

content.text: Queen of Puddings is built in three distinct stages, each of which has to be right before the next can start. The breadcrumb custard base must be fully set before the jam goes on. The jam must be spread before the meringue is piled on top. And the meringue must be baked until it is properly golden rather than just warmed. None of these stages is difficult, they just need to happen in the right order.

The breadcrumbs in the custard base are what distinguishes this from a straightforward baked egg custard. They absorb the milk and give the base a softer, more yielding texture that holds the jam and meringue without collapsing when the spoon goes in. Use fresh breadcrumbs rather than dried, dried absorb differently and make the custard dense.
A shallow baking dish, about 1.5 litres capacity. Butter it lightly before you start.
Make and bake the custard base

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Warm {{whole-milk}} of whole milk with {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, 50 g of caster sugar, and the lemon zest in a saucepan over low heat until the butter melts and the milk is steaming but not boiling. Remove from the heat and stir in {{breadcrumbs-fresh}} of fresh breadcrumbs. Leave to soak for 5 minutes, then beat in the 2 egg yolks. Pour into the buttered dish and bake for 25, 30 minutes until the custard is just set, it should tremble very slightly at the centre when shaken. Remove and cool for 10 minutes. Reduce the oven to 150°C fan.

Warm {{raspberry-jam}} of raspberry jam briefly in a small saucepan over low heat until it loosens to a spreading consistency. Spoon it gently over the surface of the custard in an even layer. Try not to press down on the custard as you spread.

Whisk the 2 egg whites in a clean bowl until they form soft peaks. Add 115 g of caster sugar a tablespoon at a time, whisking between each addition, until the meringue is thick and glossy and holds stiff peaks. Spoon over the jam in large billowing mounds rather than spreading flat, this gives a better texture after baking. Return to the oven at 150°C fan for 15 minutes until the meringue is lightly golden on the peaks.
Rest for 5 minutes before serving. Spoon into bowls, making sure each portion gets custard, jam, and meringue. Serve immediately, the meringue softens as it sits.

items.symptom: Custard base is still liquid in the centre after 30 minutes
items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or the dish was too deep
items.fix: Continue baking in 5-minute increments. The custard should be just barely set, a small wobble at the very centre is fine. A shallower dish will set faster.

items.symptom: Meringue is weeping (water pooling under it)
items.cause: Sugar was added too quickly, or the egg whites were not whisked to stiff peaks before baking
items.fix: Add the sugar one tablespoon at a time and whisk properly between additions. The meringue should be very thick and glossy before spooning. Weeping also happens if the meringue is baked too hot, keep to 150°C fan.

items.cause: The custard wasn't fully set before the jam was spread
items.fix: Make sure the custard is firmly set before spreading the jam. If it is still warm and soft, the jam will push through.

items.symptom: Meringue is soft and sticky rather than crisp on the outside
items.cause: Underbaked, or the meringue was too thin
items.fix: Pile the meringue higher, it should be in peaks, not a flat layer. Bake for a full 15 minutes. The outer surface will be lightly golden and dry to the touch.

content.text: Queen of Puddings is a Victorian invention designed to stretch basic pantry ingredients into something that looked considerably more lavish. The breadcrumb custard used stale bread that might otherwise be wasted; the jam and meringue provided colour and contrast without requiring expensive ingredients. It appears in British cookery writing from the 1860s onwards and was a regular fixture in middle-class household cooking through the Edwardian period. The pudding survived the decline of British formal pudding culture largely because its three-layer presentation remains striking enough to justify the effort.
